Introduction
Upper limb orthotic care has much higher variability than most 
other areas of orthotic practice. Howell notes that “simultaneous 
multidimensional movement of the shoulder, elbow, wrist, and 
hand across a spectrum of 26 degrees of freedom adds complexity 
and unique challenges that all need to be considered when devel -
oping and implementing the prescription criteria.”
24 Upper limb 
anatomy and biomechanics differ markedly from those of other 
segments of the body, having less soft tissue, lower force require-
ments, faster movements, increased sensory requirements, and 
more precision of movement than other limb segments. Addi -
tional consideration should be directed toward hand dominance, 
contralateral limb involvement, rehabilitation, and expected prog-
nosis.
24 The Internationational Society for Prosthetics and 
Orthotics collaborates with International Organization for Stan-
dardization (ISO) to define an orthosis as any externally applied 
device used to modify structural and functional characteristics of 
the neuromuscular skeletal system.
43 Orthosis—or alternatively, 
orthotic device—is the preferred term. 22 The terms splint and brace 
are less preferred because they imply only immobilization and do 
not suggest either improved function or restored mobility.
Principles and Indications
The objectives of upper limb orthotic applications can be classi -
fied into three major areas: protection, correction, and assistance 
with function.
• Protection: Orthotic devices can provide compressive forces 
and traction in a controlled manner to protect the impaired 
joint or body part. Restricting or preventing joint motion may 
correct alignment and prevent progressive deformity. Protec -
tive orthoses can also stabilize unstable bony components and 
promote the healing of soft tissues and bones.
• Correction: Orthoses help in correcting joint contractures and 
subluxation of joints or tendons. They assist in the prevention 
and reduction of joint deformities.
• Assistance with function: Orthoses can assist function by com-
pensating for deformity, muscle weakness, or increased muscle 
tone.
Physicians should prescribe orthotic devices based on their 
knowledge of the diagnosis, anatomy, and optimal clinical out-
comes. Other health professionals, including occupational thera -
pists and orthotists, are involved in the design and application of 
these devices.

• M any orthoses for carpal tunnel syndrome inappropriately

• Upper ex tremity robotics aid in neuroplasticity and recovery 
from motor deficits and are an exciting adjuvant to traditional 
therapy.
12
Upper Limb Orthoses and Robotics
BRIAN M. KELLY, ATUL T. PATEL, AND PAIGE PALLASKA
• Upper limb or thoses can be classified into three major areas: 
protection, correction, and assistance with function.
• T he choices of orthotic design include static, serial static,

• T he configuration of the hand bones, along with the tension of 
the muscles and ligaments in this region, contributes to the 
creation of an arch system composed of proximal transverse 
and longitudinal distal metacarpal arches, which is vital to

function related to grasp and prehension.

235Chapter 12  Upper Limb Orthoses and Robotics
Classification
Many different terms are used to describe upper limb orthotic devices. 
They are named by the joint or joints they cross, the function they 
provide (e.g., immobilization), or the condition they treat. Some are 
named by their appearance (e.g., banjo or sugar tong), and still others 
bear the name of the person who designed them (e.g., Duran).
34
Most orthoses are known by their common names (Table 12.1). 
However, such names are not fully informative, systematic, or 
universally accepted. This causes a communication barrier between 
the physician and other health professionals. Consequently, more 
systematic naming systems have been developed that classify 
orthotic devices according to anatomic region or to purpose and 
function. Table 12.1 compares the common names of several 
orthotic devices with those in three other naming systems.
The simplest naming system was developed by the ISO. It uses 
the anatomic region the orthotic device encompasses (e.g., a 
WHO is used for the wrist and hand).
45 This system, however, 
fails to define the purpose or function of the orthosis.
In 1991, the American Society of Hand Therapists published 
Splint Classification System. 1 It provides standard nomenclature 
for orthoses based on function. It classifies orthoses by character -
istics (e.g., articular or nonarticular) and location of the body 
part covered. A humeral fracture brace, for example, is identified 
as a nonarticular splint–humerus. It also identifies the direction 
of the applied force and whether the orthosis is for mobilization,

Biomechanical and Anatomic Considerations
Health care personnel involved in the fabrication and application 
of orthotic devices must have a good understanding of the biome-
chanics, anatomy, and physiologic response to tissue healing. 
T echnical and creative skills are needed to design and fabricate 
orthotic devices that patients will use and that will satisfy the goals 
of treatment. In the United States, upper limb orthotic fabrica -
tion is performed by occupational therapists, certified hand thera-
pists, orthotists, and some physical therapists. Physicians ordering 
these devices must understand the biomechanical factors involved 
in fabrication and fitting. Some of these are listed here:
• The wrist acts as the base for hand positioning and orthotic 
support except in isolated digital splinting. In the normal 
hand, the wrist’s resting posture is in slight flexion with ulnar 
deviation. The metacarpophalangeal (MCP) and interphalan -
geal (IP) joints (which include the proximal interphalangeal 
[PIP] and distal interphalangeal [DIP] joints) rest in slight 
flexion. The hand is rotated to a relaxed position of 30 to  
60 degrees. The thumb is straight and relaxed.
32
• In contrast, an impaired hand will have a different posture. The 
weight of the immobile hand, gravity, and resting muscle tension 
tend to pull the wrist into flexion. This increases tension in the 
extrinsic extensor tendons, pulling the MCP joints into hyperex-
tension. Concurrently, the tension of the extrinsic flexor tendons 
is maintained, forcing the IP joints into flexion. The metacarpal 
arch of the hand flattens and the thumb falls into adduction, 
resulting in a “claw hand” that is not functional. Prevention of 
this deformity is one goal of wrist-hand–based orthoses.
• The bone configuration of the hand and the tension of the 
muscles and ligaments in this region contribute to the creation 
of an arch system composed of the proximal transverse and 
longitudinal distal metacarpal arches. This arch system is vital 
for positioning the hand for normal function related to grasp 
and prehension. It is essential to incorporate these arches within 
the orthosis to allow maximum function and comfort.
49
• Fig. 12.1 Long arm orthosis used for cubital tunnel syndrome.
• The MCP (or MP) joint is the key for finger function. When  
MCP joints are hyperextended, the IP joints flex due to the 
tension of the flexors and the delicate balance between the finger 
extensors and flexors. Extension stability of the wrist is important 
for optimal function of the hand. The wrist should be placed in 
slight extension to maintain the length of the flexor tendon and 
improve hand function (Fig. 12.2). This position will place the 
MCP collateral ligaments on maximal stretch, preserve the 
anatomic arches of the hand, and thus oppose the development  
of a claw-hand deformity. This position is also referred to as “safe” 
or “intrinsic plus.”
42 It facilitates the weaker intrinsic motions of 
MCP flexion and IP extension, which are difficult to obtain.
• The hand is used during functional activities through basic   
prehension patterns: to pinch, to grasp, or to hook objects. 
There are two basic types of hand grips: power and precision 
(Figs. 12.3 and 12.4). For power grip, the wrist is held in dorsi-
flexion with the fingers wrapped around an object held in the 
palm (as in holding a screwdriver with a cylindric grip). The 
spheric grip is useful for holding a ball. The hook pattern is use-
ful for carrying heavy objects. For precision grip, the thumb is 
held against the tip of the index and middle fingers. Functional 
hand splinting is typically aimed at improving pinch. There are 
three types of pinch: (1) oppositional pinch (three-jaw chuck), 
(2) precision pinch, and (3) lateral key pinch.
32 It is best to splint 
toward oppositional pinch. This allows the best compromise 
between fine precision pinch and strong lateral pinch. No prac-
tical orthosis can substitute for or improve thumb adduction.
42 
When an orthosis is being made, it should be fabricated in a 
position that enhances prehension and does not force the thumb 
into a position of extension and radial abduction. Such a poor 
thumb position forces the rest of the arm to compensate.
42
• When joint range of motion (ROM) is being increased with 
splinting, the angle of pull must be perpendicular to the bony 
axis that is being mobilized.
4 Otherwise, the forces on the skin 
and underlying structures may cause injury through excessive 
pressure on the skin and deforming stresses on the underlying 
healing structures.
• The impro vement in ROM is directly proportional to the 
length of time a joint is held at its end range. 19 This is the 
principle of total end range time, which is used with static 
• Fig. 12.2 Hand and wrist are positioned to keep the metacarpophalan -
geal joints flexed and the interphalangeal joint extended with the wrist in 
slight extension. This is the “safe” or “intrinsic plus” position.

The clinically safe degree of force covers a very narrow range.
Diagnostic Categories and Orthotic Devices
Orthotic devices can be classified by the support given (or forces 
provided) to improve motion or function. The categories of splint 
design are listed in Table 12.2 (Figs. 12.5–12.20).
48
There are many common clinical conditions for which orthotic 
intervention is appropriate. This section gives a brief overview of 
the features of specific diagnoses and their commonly indicated 
type of orthosis. This is not an all-inclusive list, and the reader is 
referred to comprehensive overviews of upper limb orthotic 
devices in dedicated texts and other references.
1,9,14,20,22,23,25,42,49
Musculoskeletal Conditions
Tendonitis, Tenosynovitis, Enthesopathy, Arthritis,  
and Hypermobility
T endonitis (inflammation of the tendon), tenosynovitis (inflamma-
tion of the tendon sheaths), and enthesopathy (inflammation at a 
muscle or tendon origin or insertion) can all result from excessive 
repetitive movement or external stressors. The upper limb tendons 
most commonly involved are the wrist extensors (such as lateral 
epicondylitis) and the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis 
brevis muscles of the thumb (such as de Quervain tenosynovitis). 
The goal of fabricating an orthosis for these conditions is to im -
mobilize the affected structures to facilitate healing and decrease 
inflammation. The forearm-based thumb spica splint immobilizes 
the wrist, the carpometacarpal (CMC) joint, and the MCP joint of 
the thumb. The IP joint of the thumb does not need fixation be -
cause the affected tendons do not move this joint (Fig. 12.21).
Lateral epicondylitis is a common enthesopathy of the up -
per limb,
48 which can be treated by a tennis elbow orthosis 
(Fig. 12.22). This is a forearm band that changes the lever arm 
against which the wrist extensors pull. It puts the origin of the 
extensor muscles at rest and decreases the microtrauma from 
overuse with lifting activities. This orthotic device is a firm 
strap against which the extensors press when they are contract -
ing; it is placed approximately two fingerbreadths distal to the 
lateral epicondyle. A similar brace is used for medial epicondy -
litis (also known as golfer’s elbow) (see Chapter 36).
T rigger finger causes a snapping sensation in the volar surface of 
the digits on release of grasp. It is usually a result of trauma to the 
flexor tendon sheath of the fingers or thumb causing thickened ten-
dinous sheaths and restriction of motion. In advanced trigger finger, 
the digit can become “locked” in flexion. The goal in this condition 
is to halt the repetitive motion temporarily to allow for healing. Func-
tional use of the hand should be maintained, although the affected 
digit is immobilized (Fig. 12.23). The orthosis for trigger finger cov-
ers the proximal phalanx and the MCP joint of the involved digit. It 
decreases the tendinous excursion through the first annular pulley, at 
the base of the MCP joint, and allows the inflamed structures to rest.
Sprains
Sprains are defined as momentary subluxations with spontaneous 
reduction that result in torn ligamentous structures. Patients  
experience pain, swelling, and decreased function. Sprains require 
joint immobilization in a position of function to allow for healing 
as well as functional use. Common sprains include dislocation of 
the IP and MCP joints caused by hyperextension and are often 
seen in sports injuries (see Chapters 32, 36, and 40). For a first- or 
second-degree ligamentous tear, the goal is to protect and rest the 
area by applying functional splinting. The goal for a third-degree 
tear is to fully immobilize and approximate the ligaments.
Common orthoses used for digital sprains are finger extension 
splints that hold the PIP joint in extension but allow flexion of the 
DIP joint. This action keeps the oblique retinacular ligament and 
the terminal extensor tendon lengthened, preventing boutonnière 
deformities (Fig. 12.24) during healing. Ulnar collateral ligamentous

the body part or joint sufficiently to promote healing while

Arthritis and Hypermobility
Osteoarthritis is the most common disease affecting the j oints in 
the upper limb. Joint diseases of the hand and wrist have the most 
significant impact on function. Chronic inflammation often 
exposes these digital joints to further risk of deformity and injury. 
Orthotic devices can provide functional positioning to prevent 
further deformity and loss of use in arthritic diseases while 
protecting the joints from further injury.
1 They also provide pain 
relief and help the user stay actively engaged in purposeful 
activity, thus preventing the development of weakness.
Rheumatoid arthritis is a chronic inflammatory disease that 
primarily affects synovial joints. The most frequently affected 
joints in the upper limb are the wrist, MCP joint, and PIP joint. 
Deformities include subluxation and ulnar deviation at the MCP 
joints, subluxation and radial deviation at the wrist, and swan-
neck and boutonnière deformities of the fingers. These deformi -
ties usually progress, especially if no attempt is made to rest, and 
protect the affected joints from overuse or if medical management 
is unlikely to slow disease progression (see Chapter 32).
Several options are available for orthotic intervention for the 
rheumatoid hand. Ulnar deviation orthoses that pull the MCP 
joints toward radial deviation and increase the functional use  
of the hand are now lightweight and permit full MCP joint 
motion in flexion and extension ( Fig. 12.29). Wrist orthoses 
that provide light support for the wrist are usually well toler -
ated (see Fig. 12.25).
8 Swan-neck and boutonnière splints can 
now be made from thin, lightweight thermoplastics and are  
less bulky and more cosmetically pleasing than in the past. The 
swan-neck splint allows for flexion of the digit but blocks

Osteoarthritis, the most common form of arthritis, is primarily 
a disease of cartilage. It most commonly involves the CMC joint 
of the thumb. A hand-based thumb spica orthosis (see Figs. 12.25 
and 12.26), which offers more support to the CMC joint or 
forearm-based (see Fig. 12.16) thumb spica orthosis, can be pre-
scribed for CMC joint osteoarthritis. By limiting motion at the 
base of the thumb, the orthosis decreases pain, especially with 
pinching-type activities. Previously, a long opponens orthosis was 
thought to have better efficacy. However, a double-blind random-
ized controlled trial demonstrated no difference in pain at rest, 
pinch grip strength, or hand function.
47 A short, comfortable 
orthosis is better tolerated and improves compliance.
Hypermobility syndromes, such as Ehlers-Danlos syndrome 
(EDS), are generally characterized by joint hypermobility, skin 
hyperextensibility, and tissue fragility. Patients with EDS may 
have loose, unstable joints that are prone to frequent dislocations, 
subluxations, and pain that lead to an early onset of osteoarthritis. 
The goal is to provide stability. Silver Ring ( Fig. 12.30) offers 
several low-profile prefabricated and cosmetically attractive ortho-
ses for such patients.
Neuromuscular Conditions
Nerve Injuries
When a peripheral nerve is injured, the level and completeness  
of the injury determine the extent of the deficit incurred. For  
example, in a distal median nerve injury, a simian hand deformity 
may occur. The function most affected is thumb palmar abduction 
and opposition (Fig. 12.31). The thumb is held back in line with 
the fingers because of the pull of the extensor muscles. The goal of 
an orthotic device is to help restore lost function. The orthotic 
design holds the MCP joints in slight flexion but permits MCP 
extension. This orthosis also has a portion to position the thumb 
in palmar abduction. A median nerve lesion, proximal to the mo-
tor branches of the forearm flexors, is diagnosed if the patient is

middle fingers cannot flex at the MCP joint, and the thumb is 
usually unable to flex or oppose. This is referred to as the benedic-
tion sign. With a proximal ulnar nerve injury, the patient has wast-
ing of the hypothenar muscles of the hand: the interossei muscles 
and the two medial lumbrical muscles. The patient will have dif -

resultant flexion of the PIP joints caused by the loss of balance 
between the extrinsic and intrinsic hand muscles. This is referred

fourth and fifth MCP joints and improve function. An ulnar nerve 
palsy orthosis holds the MCP joints of the fourth and fifth fingers 
in slight flexion by using a figure-of-eight splint design. The figure-
of-eight design assists MCP flexion and permits extension of the 
MCP joints but blocks hyperextension (Fig. 12.32). This can also

Here is a word of caution in regard to using a prefabricated wrist 
orthosis for carpal tunnel syndrome: Many of these orthoses have 
an angled metal bar to hold the wrist in 45 degrees of extension 
(Fig. 12.35). This angle far exceeds the recommended 0 to 5 degrees 
of extension needed to decrease pressure in the carpal tunnel. 
Patients should be instructed to remove the metal spline, flatten it, 
and then replace it in the fabric sleeve. Usually, this type of orthosis 
should be worn for 4 to 6 weeks, with gradual weaning from the 
orthosis and return to activity with workstation modifications.
Cubital tunnel syndrome (compression of the ulnar nerve at the 
elbow) can be treated with a long arm orthosis (see Fig. 12.1) that 
holds the elbow in 45 degrees of flexion, the forearm in neutral, and 
the wrist in 0 to 5 degrees of extension with thumb and fingers free.
In patients with multiple nerve injuries or brachial plexopathy 
with essentially a flail arm, the goal for orthotic devices is to pro -
vide some functional use. One type of orthosis is an exoskeleton 
on the arm, similar to a prosthesis (Fig. 12.36). This device uses a 
shoulder harness with scapular activation to produce elbow func -
tion that is similar to scapular action in an upper limb prosthesis.

Brain Injury and Stroke
Depending on the area of brain injury and ensuing deficits, par -
ticularly if there is a change in muscle tone, orthotic devices 
should be designed to prevent deformities and help adjust muscle 
tone. Resting and positioning orthotic devices are also necessary 
to help prevent complications such as distal edema, joint sublux -
ation, and contracture formation. In upper limb paralysis, a rest-
ing hand orthosis is commonly used to position the wrist in slight 
extension, the MCP joints in slight flexion, and the IP joints in 
extension. The thumb is supported in a position between palmar 
and radial abduction. Full support of the first CMC joint prevents 
ligamentous stresses on the thumb, especially in the insensate 
hand. This thumb position uses the reflex-inhibiting posture to

no or weakened ability to place the thumb in opposition and pal-

and finger drop. The goal in this case is to enhance wrist and finger 
extension. A radial nerve palsy orthosis ( Fig. 12.34) is forearm 
based with an outrigger that holds the wrist, fingers, and thumb in 
extension and allows for flexion of the digits.

producing complete paralysis, as, for example, in median nerve

syndrome that produces an inflammatory response in the synovium 
surrounding the flexor tendons at the wrist, decreasing the blood 
supply to the median nerve. The purpose of the orthosis is to 
immobilize the wrist to minimize swelling from overuse of the 
tendons. Complete resolution of this syndrome can occur if wrist

A proximal mobile arm support can be used to enhance 
function for patients with proximal upper limb weakness, espe -
cially when the weakness is profound and the outlook for recov-

joint, this is often called a balanced forearm orthosis.

These slings restrict active motion of the shoulder by keeping  
the humerus in adduction and internal rotation and placing the  
elbow in flexion.

Spinal Cord Injury
In patients with spinal cord injury, orthotic devices are needed  
to enhance function, help with positioning, or both. The type of 
device depends on the level of injury and the extent of neurologic 
compromise. With spinal cord injury at the C1 to C3 level, the 
goal is to prevent contractures and to hold the wrist and digits in 
a position of function with a resting hand orthosis (see Fig. 12.37). 
In a C4-level injury, the goal is to use the available shoulder 
strength by providing a mobile arm support to enhance function 
as previously described. In a C5-level injury, the goal is to statically 
position the wrist in extension with a ratchet-type hinged orthotic 
device to hold devices and use the shoulder musculature for func-
tion. An orthotic device for a patient with C6 tetraplegia can  
enhance finger flexion with a tenodesis flexion effect from wrist 
extension. For example, a tenodesis splint from the Rehabilitation 
Institute of Chicago, molded from thermoplastic materials, has 
several positioning components (see Figs. 12.17 and 12.18).  
A thumb post component positions the thumb in palmar abduc -
tion. A dorsal finger piece component, which is attached with a 
static line to a volar forearm component, holds the PIP joints of 
the index and long fingers in slight flexion. When the patient  
extends the wrist, the static line pulls the fingers toward the thumb 
post. This produces a three-point pinch, allowing the patient to 
grasp an object. When the patient relaxes the wrist, the fingers 
extend passively, releasing the object. The degree of pinch varies 
depending on the strength of the wrist extensors and the degree of 
finger flexion, extension, and opposition. This custom-made ther-
moplastic tenodesis device is mainly used in training and practice. 
If a patient finds the device useful, a light metal custom-made  
tenodesis orthosis that achieves better functional restoration can be 
fabricated (see Chapter 50). An adaptive or functional-use orthosis 
promotes functional use of the upper limb with impairment result-
ing from weakness, paralysis, or loss of a body part. An example is 
the universal cuff (see Fig. 12.20), which encompasses the hand 
and holds various small items such as a fork, pen, or toothbrush, 
enhancing independence.
Orthoses for Other Injuries
Postsurgical and Postinjury Orthoses
Many types of orthoses have been developed to help stiff joints 
regain motion. Examples of such orthoses include dynamic elbow 
flexion and extension orthoses after upper arm (see Fig. 12.27) or 
elbow fracture (Fig. 12.40), dynamic wrist flexion and extension 
orthoses after a Colles fracture, and dynamic finger flexion and 
extension orthoses for stiffness after crush injuries to the hand 
(Fig. 12.41). Similar orthoses can be fabricated with a static pro -
gressive approach. Joints that have a soft end feel do well with 
dynamic orthoses. Those with a rigid end stop typically respond 
better to a static progressive approach that will maintain a con-
stant joint position while the tissue accommodates gently to the 
tension without the influence of gravity or motion.
49 Examples of 
static progressive orthoses are the Joint Jack (Fig. 12.42) or cinch 
straps and orthoses for PIP and DIP joint contractures with the 
MERiT components (see Fig. 12.13).

hand-based orthosis is determined by the need for stabilization. In

Several orthotic designs are currently used after repair of 
flexor tendon injuries. Usually, the wrist and MCP joints are 
held in flexion and the PIP and DIP joints are held in extension, 
thereby keeping the finger flexors on slack while preventing the 
development of PIP flexion contractures. T wo main programs 
are prescribed following flexor tendon repair: early passive  
motion or early active mobilization. The type of surgical proce -
dure or injury level often dictates the type of orthosis and  
program used. Both programs are designed to provide early 
mobilization by limiting the amount of tendon excursion and 
help prevent any scar adhesions that could impede functional 
gliding. Early mobilization also increases the rate of revascular -
ization and healing of the repair, increases repair strength, and 
helps remodel the gliding surface of the tendon itself.
39 For 
flexor tendon repair, the Duran orthosis is commonly used for 
manual passive flexion and extension. This orthosis statically 
positions the wrist and MCP joints in flexion and the IP joints 
in extension (Fig. 12.44). The Indiana Protocol orthosis can also 
be used ( Figs. 12.45 and 12.46). This orthotic device adds a   
tenodesis-type action splint to the componentry for specific 
early active-assisted ROM exercises. It can be used only if a 
specific surgical suture technique (with six strands) has been 
used that can withstand the load.
The form of an extensor tendon repair orthoses depends on 
the level of injury. A mallet finger injury may require only an 
off-the-shelf Stax splint, which is a static orthosis holding the 
DIP joint in full extension. A custom orthosis can be designed 
for fit and function if needed ( Fig. 12.47). A more proximal 
injury, however, needs a splint that holds the wrist statically 
in extension with dynamic extension of MCP and IP joints 
(Fig. 12.48). Such an orthosis permits active flexion of the 
MCP joints within the constraints of the orthosis to an angle 
of approximately 30 degrees. Injuries to the thumb flexor or 
extensor tendons require more specific splinting ( Fig. 12.49) 
depending on the level of the injury.
Postoperative joint replacements for the PIP , DIP , or MCP 
joints of the hand require specific splints that promote healing or 
encapsulation of the joints while preserving ROM during the 
healing phases (Fig. 12.50).

can lead to loss of functional ROM. In this case, the orthosis acts 
to prevent contractures and deformities from developing. This is 
especially important when the patient cannot voluntarily maintain

Pediatric Applications
Major reasons for the use of orthoses in the pediatric population 
include the need for functional positioning ( Fig. 12.52), normal-
ization of muscle tone (Fig. 12.53), postoperative protection, and 
positioning after surgery for a congenital deformity ( Fig. 12.54). 
Orthotic management of the child must consider the child’s age, 
developmental status, growth, and functional status. Orthoses are 
expected to last at least 1 year, so the material must be durable, 
safe, and able to accommodate for some growth (e.g., in young 
children who often chew on their hand braces). Parents should be 
educated on how to apply the orthotic and to watch for any skin 
injury related to it.
Children with abnormal tone or progressive neuromuscular 
disorders (NMDs) are at higher risk for contracture develop -
ment. A contracture is defined as lack of full active or passive 
ROM. Contractures are inevitable in some NMD conditions, 
such as Duchenne muscular dystrophy. A major rationale for 
controlling the degree of contracture development is to minimize 
the adverse effects of contractures on function. It is important to 
acknowledge that static positioning of the limbs in patients with 
weak musculature is the most important cause of contracture 
development. If they are mild, upper limb contractures may not 
affect function negatively. Stretching and ROM are mainstays to 
preserve function.
36
Children can have devastating injuries as a result of motor ve-
hicle crashes, sports injuries, and accidents. C5 to C8 lesions are 
the most common cervical cord injury for pediatrics. Such inju -
ries affect the upper limbs, and orthoses can be used to prevent 
deformity and assist with functional tasks. Static WHOs are com-
monly used in children to improve hand position for functional 
activities and to maintain ROM. Dynamic WHOs are much less 
commonly used because children are often reluctant to wear them 
for functional activities, in part because of decreased sensory feed-
back caused by the orthosis.

SECTION 2  Treatment Techniques and Special Equipment
Special Considerations
Cosmesis is often a problem for patients because they care about 
the way an orthosis looks and what it means. T o ensure patient 
compliance, the orthosis must be as cosmetically acceptable as 
possible. Patients should have every opportunity to assist in 
choosing the design and appearance. Patients often have very 
good ideas about the design of an orthosis and can suggest good 
ways to strap it into place (as long as the mechanics of the ortho -
sis are not altered).
Comfort is also important. The thinner the materials used and 
the more care the therapist takes in making a close and comfortable 
fit, the better the acceptance of the orthosis. For example, areas 
around bony prominences must be relieved to prevent pressure, 
whereas edges and joints must frequently be padded to reduce 
skin irritation. Patients with arthritis who have been taking 
corticosteroids for long periods often have fragile skin, so their 
orthoses should be padded throughout. Stockinette worn under 
an orthosis also helps, particularly with perspiration in warmer 
weather.
Orthoses can be perfectly designed and skillfully fabricated 
but are useless if not worn. The more choice and input patients 
have in the design of an orthosis, the more compliant they are 
likely to be with wearing it. The wearing schedule depends on the 
goals for the orthotic device and the patient’s tolerance for wear. 
For example, a patient with a brain injury who is “storming” (i.e., 
sweating excessively and posturing) may tolerate a resting hand 
orthosis for positioning for just 30 minutes on and 3 hours off. 
By contrast, a patient with stroke and with mild spasticity could 
wear a resting hand orthosis 2 hours on and 2 hours off during 
the day and keep it on all night. Static progressive orthosis wear 
depends on the tissue response to gentle stretching. The stretch 
should be perceived as mild, and it should never awaken the 
patient at night. In a patient with both flexion and extension 
splinting needs, the flexion orthosis can be worn 1 hour on,  
2 hours off during the day, and the extension orthosis can be 
worn at night.
A resting hand orthosis for positioning is often indicated when 
edema is present (see Fig. 12.37). However, an orthosis can also 
induce edema because of an inflammatory response caused by an 
overly aggressive stretch, particularly in a patient with increased 
tone. Special strapping techniques can often lessen this response. 
Other tissue responses are also possible. Blueness or redness of the 
digits when an orthosis is being worn tells the observer that an 
overly aggressive stretch is being applied to the shortened neuro -
vascular bundles. These structures sometimes shorten because  
of the joint contracture, in which case orthosis tension must be 
decreased and the contracture stretch should be less aggressive. 21 
Skin checks should be performed after an orthosis is removed. 
More frequent checks should occur, especially if the orthosis is 
new or recently changed. Complaints of pain or tenderness may 
signal where to examine closely. The skin is examined for abra -
sions and erythema. A blanchable lesion will lose its redness when 
pressed and is not as serious as nonblanchable lesions, which  
reflect underlying tissue injury.
Orthotic prescriptions should explain the diagnosis or problem 
to be addressed. A description of the function or motion desired 
helps to alleviate confusion. It can also facilitate discussion with 
the therapist, physician, and patient regarding the best design to 
meet mutually agreed goals. A good description can also help 
clarify misunderstandings arising from conflicting naming systems 
(see Table 12.1).
Orthotic Materials
Most orthotic materials are low-temperature thermoplastics. 
Many are known by their trademark names, such as Orthoplast, 
Aquaplast, and Orfit. 37,38 Low-temperature thermoplastics  
become soft and pliable when exposed to relatively low tem -
peratures and can be shaped in a water bath at 150°F to 180°F 
(66°C to 82°C). High-temperature thermoplastics are more 
durable but require oven heating (up to 350°F [177°C]) and 
placement over a mold to achieve the desired shape. 6 All splint -
ing materials have certain characteristics determined by the 
temperature and material properties. Some, such as Ezeform, are 
very rigid when cool. 41 Others, such as Polyform, are very drap -
able when warm. 41 Firm materials may be desirable for patients 
with increased tone, whereas drapable materials may be desir -
able when conformability is needed, as when a finger is being 
splinted. Some plastic materials have a great deal of “memory.” 
This means that they return to their original shape when re -
heated—a characteristic that can help to control costs, especially 
those incurred when serial static splinting is being provided. 37
Accessibility to three-dimensional (3D) technologies, such as 
3D scanning systems and additive manufacturing (as with 3D 
printers), allows a variety of 3D applications. Additive manufac -
turing for health care has been evolving rapidly. The availability of 
materials, technology software, and computing equipment along 
with the reduction of costs have helped to advance the develop -
ment of this technology. The promise of 3D printing offers the 
orthotist the ability to customize strength within high stress areas 
of the device and allow a customized fit. 3D printing is becoming 
more ubiquitous and is here to stay, but data on additional out -
comes and material science studies are needed on how best to 
incorporate 3D printing into mainstream manufacturing.
Upper Extremity Robotics
Robotic rehabilitation targets motor impairment and is based on 
the principles of motor skill learning, which is defined as practice-
dependent changes in motor performance. However, for motor 
learning to occur, both high doses of rehabilitation and intensive 
brain stimulation are critical. 7,11 During a traditional therapy  
session, patients typically perform about 30 repetitions in a 
45-minute session.18 However, robots are capable of consistently 
delivering a reproducible experience, and each session can consist 
of as much as 1000 repetitions. 18 This can allow the therapist to 
focus on more meaningful functional tasks. Evidence for the use 
of upper extremity therapy robots is mixed. Some findings imply 
that robotic-assisted therapy can facilitate motor recovery with 
meaningful changes in activities of daily living, but others suggest 
no difference from usual care.18 One important factor to consider 
is that the timing at which robot therapy is done also affects out -
come. After a stroke, most of the recovery happens in the first  
3 months. Studies demonstrate robotic therapy increases motor 
control in the chronic phase ( .3 months postinjury), whereas 
studies looking at acute/subacute phase ( ,3 months postinjury) 
cannot differentiate therapeutic intervention recovery from natu -
ral recovery.18
For dose-matched trials that were performed for poststroke 
rehabilitation, when conventional therapy and robotic therapy 
with the same intensity and repetitions were compared, the out -
come for robotic therapy appeared to be as effective as that of 
conventional therapy.18 Robotic rehabilitation is successful when 
it provides data and feedback to the patient. Robots must be able

Rehabilitation robots are categorized into end-effector (EE) and 
exoskeleton (Exo) types according to their mechanical structures.
15 
EE robots are connected to patients at one distal point, a nd their 
joints do not align with human joints. InMotion 226 is an example 
of one that is helpful in more impairment-based situations  
(Fig. 12.55). Exo robots resemble human limbs as they are con-

with human joint axes.

International Considerations
People with a disability are often caught in a vicious cycle of pov-
erty and disability. 10 The World Health Organization estimates 
that only 5% to 15% of people with disabilities in Third World 
countries have access to assistive devices. 4 Low-income countries 
deliver assistive technology (including orthotic devices) differently 
because of cost, availability, materials, required skilled craftsman-
ship, health care access, and infrastructure issues. These factors 
represent the biggest and most difficult challenges to delivering 
orthotic devices to people in need. T raining and retaining profes-
sionals in current orthotic principles, materials, and fabrication 
techniques is challenging. The goal is to fabricate a “universal”

another time. The availability of materials can be a challenge for 
many with limited resources. This can call for ingenuity on behalf 
of the fabricator to use local materials that might reduce cost, 
time of fabrication, or withstand the environment better than 
traditional plastics common to our appliances in the developed 
world. Donations of resources and old orthotic appliances are 
critical, although not all places have the infrastructure to deliver 
the donated items to the people in need. Recognizing the need for 
recycled orthotic devices throughout the world, many charity  
orthotic and prosthetic programs will donate old or unwanted 
assistive devices. These devices will find a second life helping indi-
viduals with limited access to care or resources.
Community-based rehabilitation is a strategy developed by the 
World Health Organization for improving disability services in 
low-income countries.13 This program empowers local people to 
serve people with disabilities in their community and includes 
teaching fabrication techniques for orthotic appliances.
Summary
This chapter provides guidelines concerning the principles for 
upper limb orthotic devices as well as various classification sys -
tems and descriptions of design categories. For an orthosis to be 
fabricated, a sound understanding of the anatomy, biomechanics, 
and tissue physiology of the upper limb is required. Persons pre -
scribing upper limb orthotic devices should have a thorough
